subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
title: Wetlands in News
focus: Key facts, geographical locations, and ecological significance of recently highlighted wetlands in India.
rationale: Groups all specific wetland locations, their geographical features (oxbow lakes, reservoirs), and associated biodiversity into a single testable block.
sectionExcerpt: Covers Gokul Reservoir, Udaipur Lake, and Gogabeel Lake in Bihar (oxbow lakes, habitats for blackbuck and migratory birds, community reserves); Kopra Jalashay in Chhattisgarh; Vembanad lake in Kerala (largest brackish wetland, shrinking surface area); and Dal Lake in J&K (hosted Khelo India water games, fed by Arrah river).
evidenceBullets: Gokul Reservoir (Buxar, Bihar) is an oxbow lake and an important habitat for Blackbuck.
Gogabeel Lake (Katihar, Bihar) is the 1st community reserve of Bihar, formed from the flow of Mahananda, Kankhar, and Ganga rivers.
Vembanad Lake (Kerala) is the largest brackish, humid tropical wetland ecosystem in Kerala, and its surface area shrank by 27% between 1917 and 1990.
Dal Lake (J&K) hosted the first Khelo India water games, is fed by the Arrah river, and is divided into Gagribal, Lokut Dal, Bod Dal, and Nagin basins.

subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
title: Coral Reefs and Coral Bleaching
focus: Coral biology, optimal growth conditions, bleaching mechanisms, conservation technologies, and global/Indian distribution.
rationale: A comprehensive ecological and geographical overview of coral reefs, making it a high-yield topic for UPSC environment questions.
sectionExcerpt: Details the symbiotic relationship between polyps and zooxanthellae, types of reefs (fringing, barrier, atoll), and optimal conditions (23-29°C, shallow clear water). Explains coral bleaching due to stress and highlights conservation technologies like Biorock and 3D-printed reefs. Maps distribution globally (Coral Triangle) and in India (Andaman & Nicobar, Lakshadweep, Gulf of Kachchh/Mannar).
evidenceBullets: Corals have a symbiotic relationship with zooxanthellae algae; unlike hard corals, soft corals lack calcium carbonate skeletons.
Optimal coral conditions include 23-29°C temperatures, clear shallow waters less than 50 meters deep, and stable salinity.
Biorock Technology uses electrical currents to stimulate coral formation via the deposition of calcium carbonate.
India's largest coral reef system is located in the Andaman and Nicobar Group of Islands.
The Coral Triangle includes the Philippines, Brunei, Malaysia, Singapore, Indonesia, Papua New Guinea, Timor-Leste, Solomon Islands, and Australia.

subtopic: Indian Wetlands and Coral Reef Conservation
title: Mangrove Ecosystems and SAIME Initiative
focus: The SAIME model for sustainable aquaculture, mangrove ecological characteristics, and mangrove distribution in India.
rationale: Combines a specific conservation initiative (SAIME) with the foundational ecology and current status of mangroves in India, forming a complete study unit.
sectionExcerpt: Discusses the Sustainable Aquaculture in Mangrove Ecosystems (SAIME) model recognized by FAO, aimed at protecting mangroves in South Asia (Sunderbans) through low-density shrimp farming. Details mangrove characteristics (pneumatophores, vivipary, carbon storage) and notes India's mangrove cover (4,991.68 sq. km) with West Bengal holding the largest share.
evidenceBullets: SAIME is a multi-stakeholder partnership promoting sustainable shrimp trade to protect mangrove ecosystems in South Asia, specifically operationalizing Integrated Mangrove Aquaculture (IMA) in the Sunderbans.
Mangroves feature unique root systems like pneumatophores and stilt roots, and exhibit vivipary where seeds germinate on maternal plants.
Mangroves act as Nature's Carbon Vault, storing 7.5 to 10 times more carbon per acre than tropical forests.
India recorded an 11.4% net increase in mangrove cover between 2001 and 2023.
West Bengal holds the largest share of India's mangroves (42.45%), followed by Gujarat (23.32%) and the Andaman & Nicobar Islands (12.39%).
